title: 2D DP
description: When the state needs TWO indices instead of one -- a 2D array (2D Arrays) of sub-answers, using Unique Paths and Minimum Path Sum on a grid.
objectives: Define a 2D DP table's meaning (dp[i][j]) before writing the recurrence
Solve Unique Paths (counting) and Minimum Path Sum (optimizing) on a grid
Recognize when a problem's state genuinely needs two dimensions

sections.text: 1D DP's state was a single position, dp[i]. Some problems -- especially grid-based ones -- need TWO coordinates to describe a state: a row AND a column. That's 2D DP, using a 2D array exactly like the grids from 2D Arrays.
2. Unique Paths: Counting Routes Through a Grid
Starting at the top-left of an m x n grid, moving only RIGHT or DOWN, how many distinct paths reach the bottom-right corner?
The number of distinct paths from the top-left corner to cell (i, j), moving only right or down.

Any path arriving at (i, j) came from EITHER directly above (i-1, j) or directly to the left (i, j-1) -- there's no other way to reach it, since only right/down moves are allowed. So dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j] + dp[i][j-1]. The entire top row and left column are base cases of exactly 1, since there's only one way to travel in a straight line.
3. Minimum Path Sum: Optimizing, Not Just Counting
The minimum total sum of a path from the top-left corner to cell (i, j), moving only right or down, adding up every grid value visited along the way.

sections.title: Tracing minPathSum on grid {{1,3,1},{1,5,1},{4,2,1}}
steps: Return dp[2][2] = 7 -- the path 1->3->1->1->1 (right,right,down,down) sums to 1+3+1+1+1=7

sections.text: Unlike Unique Paths (which just ADDS both directions), Minimum Path Sum takes the MIN of the two incoming directions, then ADDS the current cell's own value -- because we want the cheapest way to arrive, not the count of all ways.

sections.title: Signs a problem needs a 2D state
points: The problem genuinely involves a GRID, or two independent sequences being compared position by position (foreshadowing LCS, coming up soon)
A single index can't capture 'where you are' -- you need a ROW and a COLUMN (or two separate sequence positions) simultaneously
The recurrence naturally references dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1], or dp[i-1][j-1] -- neighboring cells in a 2D sense, not just 'one or two positions back' in a line

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Forgetting to initialize the ENTIRE top row and left column as base cases before the main double loop -- both are needed since the recurrence relies on both directions.

Confusing counting problems (sum both directions, like Unique Paths) with optimization problems (take min/max of both directions, like Minimum Path Sum) -- they use the same 2D SHAPE but different combining logic.
Iterating i and j in the wrong order, computing dp[i][j] before dp[i-1][j] or dp[i][j-1] are actually ready.
Assuming every grid problem needs 2D DP -- some can be simplified; always check whether the state genuinely needs two independent dimensions.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is the recurrence for Unique Paths, and why?
questions.answer: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j] + dp[i][j-1], because any path arriving at (i,j) with only right/down moves allowed must have come from directly above or directly to the left -- summing both counts every distinct path.

questions.question: How does Minimum Path Sum's recurrence differ from Unique Paths', despite both being grid-based DP?
questions.answer: Minimum Path Sum takes dp[i][j] = grid[i][j] + min(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]) -- using MIN to find the cheapest incoming path and adding the current cell's cost, whereas Unique Paths uses SUM to count all possible paths rather than optimize a cost.

questions.question: What signals that a DP problem needs a 2D state instead of 1D?
questions.answer: When a single index can't fully describe 'where you are' -- typically a grid problem needing a row and column, or a problem comparing two independent sequences position by position, where the recurrence naturally references neighboring cells like dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1], or dp[i-1][j-1].

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: 2D DP uses a table dp[i][j] when a problem's state needs two coordinates, common in grid problems. Unique Paths counts routes to (i,j) with dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j] + dp[i][j-1], since any path arriving there came from directly above or directly left. Minimum Path Sum instead optimizes cost with dp[i][j] = grid[i][j] + min(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]) -- same 2D shape, but min instead of sum since it's minimizing rather than counting. Both need their entire top row and left column initialized as base cases before the main double loop runs. Recognizing whether a problem needs 1D or 2D state is the key first step, just as with 1D DP.

sections.title: Trick to Remember
sections.text: 2D DP = a grid where each cell's answer depends on its NEIGHBOR ABOVE and NEIGHBOR TO THE LEFT. Counting problems ADD those neighbors. Optimizing problems take the BEST (min/max) of those neighbors, then add the current cell's own cost.

sections.title: Quick Understanding Check
questions: What is Unique Paths' recurrence, and why does it sum two directions?
How does Minimum Path Sum's recurrence differ from Unique Paths'?
What base cases must be set before the main double loop in these grid DP problems?
What signals a problem needs a 2D DP state rather than 1D?

sections.title: Unique Paths
sections.problemStatement: A robot on an m x n grid starts top-left, can move only right or down, and wants to reach the bottom-right. Return the number of unique paths.
hints: dp[r][c] = dp[r-1][c] + dp[r][c-1] (paths from above plus paths from the left).
First row and first column each have exactly 1 path (straight line).

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: 2D tabulation initializing the first row and column to 1, then filling by summing top and left neighbors.

sections.title: Unique Paths II
sections.problemStatement: Same as Unique Paths, but some grid cells are obstacles (marked 1) that cannot be entered.

examples.explanation: The obstacle at center forces exactly 2 distinct routes around it.

hints: Same recurrence as Unique Paths, but dp[r][c] = 0 whenever the cell itself is an obstacle.
The first row/column of 1s is broken as soon as an obstacle appears -- cells after it (in that row/column) become unreachable (0).

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Tabulate as in Unique Paths, but check obstacleGrid[r][c] == 1 first and set dp[r][c] = 0 in that case (overriding the sum).

sections.title: Minimum Path Sum
sections.problemStatement: Given an m x n grid of non-negative numbers, find a path from top-left to bottom-right (moving only right or down) that minimizes the sum of numbers along it.
hints: First row/column can only be reached one way -- accumulate the running sum.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Tabulate minimum cost to reach each cell, initializing the first row/column as cumulative sums, then taking the min of top/left plus the current cell's value.

sections.title: Triangle
sections.problemStatement: Given a triangle array, return the minimum path sum from top to bottom, where each step moves to an adjacent index on the row below.
hints: Work BOTTOM-UP: dp[i] at the last row equals that row's values directly.
For row above, dp[i] = triangle[row][i] + min(dp[i], dp[i+1]) using the row below's already-computed values -- this lets you reuse a single 1D array.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Bottom-up 1D DP: start with the last row's values, then for each row above, update dp[i] = triangle[row][i] + min(dp[i], dp[i+1]) using the row below's results still sitting in the array.

sections.title: Minimum Falling Path Sum
sections.problemStatement: Given an n x n matrix, return the minimum sum of a falling path (from any cell in the first row, moving to a cell directly below or diagonally adjacent below in the next row, down to the last row).

examples.explanation: Path 1 (row0,col1) -> 5 (row1,col1, directly below) -> 7 (row2,col0, diagonally below-left) sums to 1+5+7 = 13 -- the minimum falling path.

hints: dp[r][c] = matrix[r][c] + min of the up-to-3 valid cells directly above or diagonally above in the previous row.
Guard against out-of-bounds columns at the matrix edges.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Tabulate row by row: dp[r][c] = matrix[r][c] + min(dp[r-1][c-1], dp[r-1][c], dp[r-1][c+1]), treating out-of-bounds neighbors as infinity. First row's dp equals the matrix values directly.
